meta.title: Protocol Buffers & gRPC
meta.description: Protobuf schema design, wire compatibility, gRPC API design

items.text: Are field numbers stable and never reused for different semantics?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Protobuf field numbers are part of the wire format. Once assigned, a field number must never be changed or reused for a different field, even after the original field is removed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Field numbers are how protobuf identifies fields on the wire. Reusing a number means old data will be deserialized into the wrong field, causing silent data corruption that is extremely hard to diagnose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that no existing field numbers have been changed. If fields were removed, verify their numbers are reserved. New fields should use the next available number.
baseContent.exampleComment: Field number 3 was previously used for 'legacy_status'. Reusing it for 'priority' will cause existing serialized data to deserialize incorrectly. Please use the next available number.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Field numbers are forever. Never reuse them. Reserve removed field numbers.

items.text: Are deprecated fields marked with `reserved` to prevent reuse?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When removing a field from a proto message, mark both the field number and field name as reserved to prevent accidental reuse in the future.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without reserved, a future developer might unknowingly reuse the field number or name, causing wire incompatibility with existing serialized data or old clients.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for removed fields. Are the field numbers and names added to the reserved statement? Is there a comment explaining what the field was?
baseContent.exampleComment: This field removal does not include a reserved statement. Future developers might reuse field number 5, causing data corruption. Please add reserved 5 and reserved 'old_field_name'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always reserve removed field numbers and names. It is a safety net for future development.

items.text: Are field names in `snake_case` as per protobuf convention?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Protobuf field names should use lower_snake_case as per the official style guide. The protobuf compiler generates appropriate casing for each target language.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming ensures generated code follows each language's conventions automatically. Java gets camelCase getters, Python gets snake_case - but only if the proto uses snake_case.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new field names in .proto files. Are they in lower_snake_case? Are there any camelCase or UPPER_CASE field names?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field 'userId' should be 'user_id' per protobuf style conventions. The protobuf compiler will generate the appropriate getUserId() getter in Java.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use lower_snake_case for proto field names. Let the compiler handle language-specific casing.

items.text: Are enums used with an `UNSPECIFIED = 0` default value?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every proto3 enum should have an UNSPECIFIED = 0 value as its first entry. This serves as the default when no value is explicitly set.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In proto3, unset enum fields default to 0. Without an UNSPECIFIED value at 0, the default silently maps to a real business value, which can cause incorrect behavior when clients forget to set the field.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check enum definitions. Does each one have a _UNSPECIFIED = 0 value? Is the 0 value truly a sentinel and not a valid business state?
baseContent.exampleComment: This enum starts with ACTIVE = 0. If a client does not set the status field, it will default to ACTIVE silently. Add STATUS_UNSPECIFIED = 0 as the first value.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use UNSPECIFIED = 0 as the default enum value. It makes unset fields detectable.

items.text: Are `oneof` fields used where exactly one of several fields should be set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use oneof when a message should contain exactly one of several mutually exclusive fields. This makes the constraint explicit in the schema.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without oneof, nothing prevents setting multiple mutually exclusive fields simultaneously. Oneof enforces mutual exclusivity at the wire level and generates type-safe accessor code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for groups of fields where only one should be set at a time. Could they be modeled as a oneof? Are existing oneof fields being used correctly?
baseContent.exampleComment: The payment_method fields (card_number, bank_account, wallet_id) are mutually exclusive. Modeling them as a oneof would enforce this constraint at the schema level.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use oneof for mutually exclusive fields. It encodes business constraints in the schema.

items.text: Are nested message types used to scope closely related messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use nested message types to scope related messages within their parent, avoiding namespace pollution and making relationships explicit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flat proto files with dozens of top-level messages become hard to navigate. Nesting communicates that a message is only used within the context of its parent, reducing cognitive overhead.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new message types. Are there messages that are only used as fields of one parent message? Could they be nested inside the parent?
baseContent.exampleComment: The OrderItem message is only used inside Order. Nesting it as Order.Item would make the relationship explicit and reduce top-level clutter.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Nest message types that belong exclusively to a parent. It communicates scope and ownership.

items.text: Are `repeated` fields used instead of separate count + values patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use repeated fields for lists of items instead of numbered fields like item_1, item_2, item_3. Repeated fields are the protobuf way to represent arrays.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Numbered fields create a fixed maximum, require schema changes to grow, and generate verbose code. Repeated fields are flexible, efficient, and idiomatic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for patterns of numbered fields that represent a collection. Could they be consolidated into a single repeated field?
baseContent.exampleComment: The fields tag_1 through tag_5 should be a single repeated string tags field. This allows any number of tags without schema changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use repeated fields for collections. Avoid numbered field patterns like field_1, field_2.

items.text: Are `google.protobuf.Timestamp` and `google.protobuf.Duration` used instead of raw integers for time?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use google.protobuf.Timestamp for points in time and google.protobuf.Duration for time spans, rather than custom int64 or string fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Well-known types provide standardized semantics, cross-language conversion utilities, and JSON serialization support. Custom timestamp fields require documentation and manual conversion in every language.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for int64 or string fields that represent timestamps or durations. Could they use the well-known types instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: The field 'created_at_millis' as int64 would be better as google.protobuf.Timestamp. It provides standardized semantics and automatic conversion to Java Instant.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use well-known types for timestamps and durations. They provide standardized semantics across languages.

items.text: Are `google.protobuf.FieldMask` used for partial update APIs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use google.protobuf.FieldMask in update requests to specify which fields should be modified, enabling partial updates without overwriting unset fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without FieldMask, update operations must either send the entire object (risk overwriting concurrent changes) or add a separate boolean for each field (verbose and error-prone).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check update RPCs. Do they use FieldMask to specify which fields to update? Are unset fields preserved rather than zeroed out?
baseContent.exampleComment: This UpdateUser RPC takes the full User message but cannot distinguish between 'set name to empty string' and 'do not change name'. Adding a FieldMask solves this.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use FieldMask for partial updates. It makes intent explicit and prevents accidental overwrites.

items.text: Are `bytes` fields used for opaque binary data, not `string`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use bytes fields for opaque binary data (files, encrypted content, hashes) and string fields for human-readable text. String fields must be valid UTF-8 in proto3.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Putting binary data in a string field causes UTF-8 validation failures, silent data corruption, and serialization errors. Conversely, using bytes for text loses the UTF-8 guarantee.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new fields. Are binary payloads stored in bytes fields? Are text fields using string? Is base64 encoding used unnecessarily?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'file_content' field is declared as string but stores binary data. This should be bytes to avoid UTF-8 encoding issues.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use bytes for binary data, string for text. They have different encoding guarantees on the wire.

items.text: Is the change backward compatible? Can old readers still parse new messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Backward compatibility means old clients can still read messages written by new code. New fields should have safe defaults, and removed fields should be reserved.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In distributed systems, not all services upgrade simultaneously. If a new message version cannot be read by old clients, rolling deployments will fail and require a coordinated big-bang release.
baseContent.howToVerify: Would an old client reading a new message get sensible behavior? Are new fields optional with safe defaults? Are removed fields reserved?
baseContent.exampleComment: Adding this required field breaks backward compatibility. Old clients will not set it, and new code that expects it will fail. Consider making it optional with a default behavior.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: New fields must be optional with safe defaults. Always think about what old clients will see.

items.text: Is the change forward compatible? Can new readers still parse old messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Forward compatibility means new clients can read messages written by old code. New code should handle the absence of new fields gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When rolling out changes, new code will receive messages from old producers that lack new fields. If new code crashes or behaves incorrectly on missing fields, the rollout fails.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new code handle messages without the new fields? Are there null checks or default values for new fields? Can the system function if the new field is never set?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new code assumes the 'priority' field is always set, but old producers will not include it. Add a default value check: if unset, treat as NORMAL priority.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: New code must work correctly with old messages. Handle missing fields with sensible defaults.

items.text: Are required fields avoided (proto3 doesn't support them, proto2 they're dangerous)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto3 removed the required keyword. In proto2, required fields should be avoided because they make schema evolution dangerous.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Required fields cannot be removed without breaking all existing clients. They make it impossible to evolve the schema gradually, forcing big-bang migrations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for 'required' keyword in proto2 files. In proto3, check that code does not treat fields as implicitly required by crashing when they are absent.
baseContent.exampleComment: This proto2 message uses required fields. If we ever need to remove them, all clients must update simultaneously. Consider migrating to proto3 optional fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid required fields. They create permanent contracts that cannot be safely evolved.

items.text: Are field numbers not changed for existing fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Existing field numbers must never be changed. The field number is how protobuf identifies fields on the wire, not the field name.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Changing a field number is equivalent to deleting the old field and creating a new one. All existing serialized data becomes unreadable for that field, causing silent data loss.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare the proto diff carefully. Has any existing field's number changed? Even a swap between two fields is a breaking change.
baseContent.exampleComment: This diff changes the field number of 'customer_id' from 2 to 5. This is a wire-breaking change - all existing serialized data will lose this field. Please revert the field number.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Field numbers are permanent. Changing one is a wire-breaking change that corrupts data.

items.text: Are field types not changed in incompatible ways (e.g., `int32` to `string`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changing a field's type can break wire compatibility. Some changes are safe (int32 to int64), but many are not (string to int32, message to enum).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incompatible type changes cause deserialization errors or silent data corruption. A string field changed to int32 will fail to parse existing serialized data entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for field type changes in the proto diff. Consult the protobuf compatibility table for safe type changes. When in doubt, add a new field instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: Changing 'amount' from string to int64 is a wire-breaking change. Existing serialized data has this as a string. Consider adding a new field 'amount_cents' instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Do not change field types. Add a new field and deprecate the old one.

items.text: If a field is removed, is the field number `reserved`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When removing a field from a proto message, use the reserved keyword for both the field number and name to prevent future reuse.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without reserved, a future developer might accidentally assign the same field number to a new field, causing deserialization of old data into the wrong field type - silent data corruption.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check proto diffs for removed fields. Is the field number added to a reserved statement? Is the field name also reserved?
baseContent.exampleComment: Field 4 (legacy_notes) was removed but not reserved. Please add 'reserved 4; reserved "legacy_notes";' to prevent future reuse.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reserve removed field numbers and names. It prevents accidental reuse and data corruption.

items.text: Is the `.proto` file's `package` declaration correct and consistent?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto files should have a package declaration that matches the directory structure and follows the organization's naming convention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The package prevents naming collisions between message types and affects the generated Java package. An incorrect package can cause compilation errors or put generated code in the wrong location.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that the package matches the directory path. Verify it follows the convention (e.g., com.company.service.v1). Check java_package option if different from the proto package.
baseContent.exampleComment: The proto package is 'order' but this file is in the 'com/company/order/v1/' directory. The package should be 'com.company.order.v1' to match.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Proto package declarations should match directory structure and set java_package explicitly.

items.text: Are `import` statements using the correct paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto import paths should be correct and use the appropriate import style. Use relative paths from the proto root, not absolute filesystem paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect import paths cause compilation failures. Using the wrong import style can break builds in other environments or when proto files are moved.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import statements in proto files. Do they resolve correctly? Are they using paths relative to the proto root? Are public imports used only when intentional?
baseContent.exampleComment: This import uses an absolute path that only works on your machine. Change it to a path relative to the proto root directory.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use proto root-relative import paths. They work consistently across environments.

items.text: Is the generated Java code used correctly (using builders, not constructing with `new`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto-generated Java classes are immutable. Use the Builder pattern to create and modify messages. Never try to set fields directly on a built message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Generated proto messages are immutable by design. Misusing the generated API leads to compilation errors, runtime exceptions, or incorrect patterns like creating messages without builders.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that proto messages are created using the builder pattern. Are toBuilder/build calls used for modifications? Is the generated API used idiomatically?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code creates a proto message by calling new Order() directly. Proto messages should be created using Order.newBuilder().setId(...).build().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
Order order = Order.newBuilder()
    .setId("123")
    .setCustomerId("cust-456")
    .setStatus(OrderStatus.ORDER_STATUS_ACTIVE)
    .build();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use builders to create proto messages. Generated messages are immutable.

items.text: Are `.toBuilder()` and `.build()` patterns used for modifications?

baseContent.whatItMeans: To modify an existing proto message, use toBuilder() to get a builder with the current values, modify it, and call build() to get a new immutable message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Since proto messages are immutable, the toBuilder/build pattern is the only safe way to create a modified copy. Alternatives like reflection or serialization tricks are fragile and defeat type safety.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for proto message modifications. Is toBuilder() used to create modified copies? Are there anti-patterns like building a new message from scratch when a copy-and-modify would be clearer?
baseContent.exampleComment: Instead of building a new Order from scratch, use existingOrder.toBuilder().setStatus(COMPLETED).build() to preserve all other fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use toBuilder() to modify existing messages. It preserves all fields you do not explicitly change.

items.text: Are `hasField()` checks used before accessing optional fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use hasField() to check if a message field is set before accessing it. In proto3, unset message fields return a default instance, which may not be distinguishable from an intentionally empty message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without hasField(), you cannot distinguish between 'field was explicitly set to default value' and 'field was never set'. This matters for optional fields and nested messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check accesses to optional and message-type fields. Are hasField() checks used where the distinction between unset and default matters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code accesses order.getShippingAddress() without checking hasShippingAddress() first. For orders without shipping (like digital products), this returns a default Address, not null.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use hasField() for message-type fields to distinguish between unset and default instances.

items.text: Is `.getDefaultInstance()` used instead of `null` for absent messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Proto.getDefaultInstance() instead of null when you need an empty or absent proto message. Proto APIs are designed around default instances, not null.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Passing null where a proto message is expected causes NullPointerExceptions. The default instance is a valid, empty message that works safely with all proto operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for null checks on proto message parameters. Could getDefaultInstance() be used instead? Are there places where null proto messages are passed or returned?
baseContent.exampleComment: This method returns null when there is no order. Return Order.getDefaultInstance() instead - it is safer and works with all proto operations like merging.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use getDefaultInstance() instead of null for proto messages. It eliminates NullPointerExceptions.

items.text: Are proto messages not used as map keys (they're mutable)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Proto messages should not be used as keys in HashMaps or elements in HashSets. Their equals/hashCode behavior may not match expectations and can change between proto library versions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto message equality is complex (unknown fields, default values, float precision). Using them as map keys leads to subtle bugs where lookups fail even for logically equivalent messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for proto types used as Map keys or Set elements. Extract a meaningful key (like the ID field) instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using the full Order proto as a map key is fragile. Extract order.getId() as the key instead - it is stable, predictable, and efficient.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
Map<String, List<Shipment>> orderShipments = new HashMap<>();
orderShipments.put(order.getId(), shipments);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use proto messages as map keys. Extract a stable identifier field instead.

items.text: Is serialization/deserialization done with the correct methods (`toByteArray()`, `parseFrom()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the correct serialization methods: toByteArray()/parseFrom() for binary, JsonFormat for JSON. Do not use Java serialization (Serializable) on proto messages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Proto's binary format is compact, fast, and wire-compatible. Java serialization is slow, bloated, and not compatible across proto library versions. Using the wrong method defeats proto's advantages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check how proto messages are serialized. Is toByteArray()/parseFrom() used for binary? Is JsonFormat used for JSON? Is Java's ObjectOutputStream being used accidentally?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code serializes the proto using ObjectOutputStream. Use order.toByteArray() for binary or JsonFormat.printer() for JSON instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use proto's own serialization methods. Never use Java serialization for proto messages.

items.text: Are RPC methods named with verbs describing the action (`CreateUser`, `ListOrders`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RPC method names should use verbs that describe the action: CreateOrder, GetUser, ListProducts, UpdateConfig, DeleteSession.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent verb-based naming makes APIs intuitive and predictable. Developers can guess method names without reading docs. It also aligns with Google's API design guidelines.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check RPC method names. Do they start with standard verbs (Get, List, Create, Update, Delete, Batch)? Are custom methods using descriptive verb phrases?
baseContent.exampleComment: The RPC 'OrderSubmission' should be named 'SubmitOrder' using a verb-first pattern. This aligns with the standard API naming convention.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name RPCs with verbs: Create, Get, List, Update, Delete. The name is the action.

items.text: Are request/response messages appropriately structured and not overly generic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each RPC should have its own dedicated request and response message types, even if they seem similar to other RPCs. Do not reuse generic request/response types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dedicated message types allow each RPC to evolve independently. Shared types create coupling - adding a field for one RPC leaks it to all others that share the type.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each RPC has its own request/response types named after the RPC (e.g., CreateOrderRequest/CreateOrderResponse). Are any types shared across unrelated RPCs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The GenericRequest type is shared across three RPCs. Each should have its own request type so they can evolve independently.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One request/response type per RPC. Dedicated types enable independent evolution.

items.text: Are pagination fields included for list endpoints (`page_token`, `page_size`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List RPCs should include pagination support with page_size and page_token fields in the request, and next_page_token in the response.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without pagination, list endpoints return unbounded results that can overwhelm memory, timeout, or crash the service. Even if the dataset is small today, it may grow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check List RPCs. Do they have page_size and page_token in the request? Is next_page_token in the response? Is there a maximum page_size enforced server-side?
baseContent.exampleComment: The ListOrders RPC has no pagination fields. Even if the result set is small now, adding page_size and page_token future-proofs the API.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always include pagination in List RPCs. Data grows, and unbounded lists eventually break.

items.text: Are idempotency keys used for mutating operations where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mutating operations (Create, Update, Delete) should support idempotency keys so that retried requests do not create duplicate resources or apply changes twice.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Network failures and retries are inevitable. Without idempotency, a retried CreateOrder could create two orders. Idempotency keys let the server detect and deduplicate retries safely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mutating RPCs. Do Create requests include a request_id or idempotency_key field? Is the server deduplicating based on this key?
baseContent.exampleComment: The CreatePayment RPC has no idempotency key. If a client retries after a timeout, it could charge the customer twice. Add a request_id field for deduplication.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add idempotency keys to mutating operations. Retries must be safe.

items.text: Are error codes/status codes used correctly (gRPC status codes, HTTP status codes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC status codes (OK, NOT_FOUND, INVALID_ARGUMENT, etc.) and HTTP status codes should be used correctly and consistently to communicate the nature of errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Correct status codes enable proper error handling by clients, monitoring, and alerting. Using INTERNAL for everything hides the distinction between client errors and server errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling in RPC implementations. Are the right gRPC status codes used? Is NOT_FOUND used for missing resources? INVALID_ARGUMENT for bad input? INTERNAL only for unexpected server errors?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns INTERNAL status for invalid input. Use INVALID_ARGUMENT (or 400 Bad Request) so the client knows it is their error and does not retry.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use specific status codes. INVALID_ARGUMENT for bad input, NOT_FOUND for missing resources, INTERNAL only for bugs.

items.text: Are API changes documented in relevant API docs or changelogs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes should be reflected in API documentation, changelogs, or migration guides so that consumers know about new fields, changed behavior, or deprecations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented API changes surprise consumers and erode trust. Documentation is the contract between the API provider and its consumers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR includes documentation updates. Are new fields documented with comments in the proto file? Is there a changelog entry? Are breaking changes called out?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new 'priority' field to the CreateOrder RPC but the API docs are not updated. Consumers will not know about this new capability.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document API changes in proto comments and changelogs. Consumers depend on your documentation.

items.text: Are timeouts and deadlines set appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RPC calls should have explicit timeouts/deadlines. Without them, a hung downstream service can hold connections indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing timeouts turn partial failures into total failures. A single hung RPC can exhaust thread pools and cascade through the entire system. Timeouts are circuit breakers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check gRPC client configurations. Are deadlines set for outgoing calls? Are server-side timeouts configured? Are timeouts propagated across service boundaries?
baseContent.exampleComment: This gRPC call has no deadline set. If the downstream service hangs, this thread will block indefinitely. Add a deadline of 5-10 seconds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// No timeout - can hang forever
OrderResponse response = orderStub.createOrder(request);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set deadlines on RPC calls. No timeout means infinite wait on failure.

items.text: Is input validation done early and comprehensively?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate all input at the API boundary before processing. Check for required fields, valid ranges, format constraints, and business rules.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Early validation provides clear error messages to callers and prevents invalid data from propagating through the system. Late validation causes confusing errors deep in business logic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check RPC handler methods. Is input validated before business logic runs? Are validation errors returned with specific field information? Are all edge cases covered?
baseContent.exampleComment: This handler processes the order before validating the input. If customer_id is empty, the error comes from the database layer instead of a clear INVALID_ARGUMENT response.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate early, fail fast. Clear validation errors at the boundary save debugging time.
